candidates.method_summary: Two-stage real-to-sim-to-real framework. Stage 1: pretrain a motion-tracking policy in sim on retargeted human mocap. Stage 2: collect ~20 minutes of real-robot rollouts, train a 'delta action' residual model that maps sim state + sim action → real-world correction, then fine-tune the policy in sim augmented with the delta action model. Evaluates IsaacGym→IsaacSim, IsaacGym→Genesis, and IsaacGym→real Unitree G1 transfers. Enables agile whole-body skills (Kungfu kicks, jumping) not reachable with SysID or DR alone.
limitations: Delta model is task-agnostic but embodiment-specific
Only upper-body-dominant agile motions reported — manipulation not covered

candidates.method_summary: Dual-agent RL framework for humanoid loco-manipulation under external end-effector forces. Lower-body agent handles locomotion under disturbance; upper-body agent tracks end-effector pose with implicit force compensation. candidates.title: FAST: Efficient Action Tokenization for Vision-Language-Action Models
candidates.method_summary: FAST+: universal robot action tokenizer using discrete cosine transform (DCT) based compression. Trained on 1 M real robot trajectories as a black-box tokenizer. Enables autoregressive VLA training on dexterous tasks like laundry folding, table bussing, grocery packing — tasks where prior discretized VLAs (RT-2 style) collapsed. Combined with π0, scales to 10k hours of data and matches diffusion VLA performance 5× faster.
limitations: DCT basis may not be optimal for all action distributions

candidates.title: FAST: Efficient Action Tokenization for Vision-Language-Action Models
candidates.method_summary: FAST+: universal robot action tokenizer using discrete cosine transform (DCT) based compression. Trained on 1 M real robot trajectories as a black-box tokenizer. Enables autoregressive VLA training on dexterous tasks like laundry folding, table bussing, grocery packing — tasks where prior discretized VLAs (RT-2 style) collapsed. Combined with π0, scales to 10k hours of data and matches diffusion VLA performance 5× faster.
limitations: DCT basis may not be optimal for all action distributions

candidates.title: FastTD3: Simple, Fast, and Capable Reinforcement Learning for Humanoid Control
candidates.method_summary: Off-policy TD3 variant combining parallel simulation + large-batch updates + distributional critic + tuned hyperparameters. Solves HumanoidBench suite in <3 h on single A100, outperforming PPO on rough-terrain DR tasks. Signals the return of off-policy algorithms to legged-robot RL that had been PPO-dominated since 2021.
limitations: No real-robot transfer demonstration in the paper

From Experts to a Generalist: Toward General Whole-Body Control for Humanoid Robots
candidates.method_summary: Introduces the BumbleBee (BB) expert-generalist framework for humanoid whole-body control. Addresses the challenge that general agile whole-body control struggles across highly varied behaviors due to conflicting control requirements and mismatched data distributions. Uses an autoencoder-based clustering method to group similar motions into clusters; trains expert policies within each cluster, refined with real-world data; then distills these experts into a unified generalist controller. Combines motion clustering with sim-to-real adaptation in a single pipeline.
limitations: Relies on per-cluster experts already trained
Distillation may drop edge-case skills across cluster boundaries

GR00T N1: An Open Foundation Model for Generalist Humanoid Robots
candidates.method_summary: NVIDIA Isaac GR00T N1: open-weights dual-system VLA foundation model for humanoids. System 2 is a 1.34B-parameter VLM interpreting images + language; System 1 is a diffusion transformer generating continuous joint commands at high rate. Trained on heterogeneous mix: real robot trajectories, human videos (cross-embodiment via latent action), synthetic trajectories from Isaac Sim. Released 2.2B total-parameter checkpoint. Reports strong few-shot adaptation on Fourier GR-1, Unitree H1, and 1X Neo.
limitations: System 2 VLM runs off-robot or on heavy GPU; latency 100+ ms
Diffusion transformer still requires per-embodiment fine-tune for best results
Latent-action supervision from video is noisy — quantitative contribution small

candidates.title: Learning Sim-to-Real Humanoid Locomotion in 15 Minutes
candidates.method_summary: Demonstrates that off-policy RL — long assumed unstable for humanoid sim-to-real at scale — can train a deployable humanoid locomotion policy in ~15 minutes on a single RTX 4090 GPU. Uses FastSAC and FastTD3 (not PPO) with thousands of parallel environments, carefully tuned design choices, and minimalist reward functions. Demonstrated on Unitree G1 and Booster T1. Also reports rapid training of whole-body human-motion tracking policies. Contribution is a stability + throughput result for off-policy humanoid RL, companion-line to the same author's `seo2025fasttd3` paper (Younggyo Seo is first author on both).
limitations: Single-skill focus per training run — multi-skill generalist policy not demonstrated in this paper

candidates.title: Humanoid Locomotion as Next Token Prediction

Trains on mixed data: simulated rollouts, neural-network-generated trajectories, prior MPC rollouts, and even human YouTube videos (after retargeting). Zero-shot deployment on Digit in outdoor Berkeley streets.

Did not scale to manipulation in this paper

limitations: Ankle-specific rewards require tuning per embodiment

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: papers.json: 'Isaac Gym → MuJoCo drift under 8% in joint torques; zero-shot walking on both XBot platforms.' Book §5.3 claim matches. Verifiable via arXiv 2404.05695 abstract. Tier-1 matching supported.

Cassie Bipedal Robot and the ATRIAS Lineage
candidates.venue: Agility Robotics / Oregon State (ATRIAS → Cassie → Digit)
candidates.method_summary: Cassie is a research biped built on SLIP (spring-loaded inverted pendulum) principles: centralize mass in torso, minimize leg inertia. Leaf springs are located at shin pitch (q5) and tarsus pitch (q6) joints (the actuated toe is at q7) — NOT at the ankle as shorthand often implies. ATRIAS-descended design. Lineage runs ATRIAS (2014) → Cassie (2017) → Digit (2020, Agility). The passive-dynamics bet distinguishes the Hurst lineage from Kim/MIT's QDD lineage.
limitations: Leaf-spring compliance at shin/tarsus limits some reactive whole-body control

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: Primary source (Grizzle group arXiv 1809.07279 'Feedback Control of a Cassie Bipedal Robot'): Cassie's passive springs are at the SHIN PITCH (q5) and TARSUS PITCH (q6) joints — NOT at the 'ankle' (q7 is actuated toe) as book-writer §4.5 states. Proposed fix sent: replace '발목의 수동 스프링' with '정강이·tarsus 관절의 수동 판스프링'. Numerical claims (mass ~30 kg, leg mass ~10 kg, 2 passive spring joints/leg) are correct.

candidates.title: Learning Agile and Dynamic Motor Skills for Legged Robots

Key innovation is the actuator network: a neural net trained on real ANYmal motor data (joint state history → joint torque) that replaces the idealized actuator model in simulation, closing a major sim-to-real gap. Uses TRPO with a stochastic policy + randomized dynamics (mass, link length, COM offsets). Recovers from falls and runs at 1.6 m/s, up from 0.8 m/s of the shipped controller.

CORRECTIONS (book-writer §2.3 has 2 wrong numbers): (a) Paper's actual DDP iteration cost is ~50 ms (§II.C), NOT 1 ms — the 1 ms is only the line-search inner-loop iteration. (b) Paper's MPC replan rate is ~20 Hz fast-time (50 ms per iteration), NOT 200 Hz — the 200 Hz is the onboard HRP-2 PD position-control loop that consumes the MPC output. Paper explicitly states 'one iLQR iteration (around 50ms in the experiments) is not negligible with respect to the robot control rate (typically around 5ms)'. Trajectory time step is 20 ms (§IV.B). papers.json's `koenemann2022whole.quantitative_results` conflates these rates and is unreliable. Bibtex key year 'koenemann2022whole' is also off from publication year 2015 — cosmetic, not a factcheck issue. **RESOLVED 2026-04-24**: book-writer applied verbatim prescription in §2.3 (27-DoF HRP-2, 50 ms DDP iteration, 12-core offboard, ≈20 Hz fast-time, 200 Hz onboard, 20 ms trajectory time step — all primary-source-verified via Koenemann IROS 2015). BONUS: §2.4 new sentence cites Koenemann 2015 directly for the capture-point-in-MPC-cost-term claim (whole-body reaching experiment, 'keep CP at center of support polygon' cost term). Book-writer softened the claim 2026-04-24: §1.5 now reads 'approximately 1 kHz with roughly 30 ms of planning latency' — defensible hedge. Accepted as partial_verified. RESOLVED (via softening).

candidates.method_summary: MIT Cheetah design-principles paper: custom outer-rotor BLDC + coaxial planetary gear, low-impedance actuation, efficient leg structure. Pre-Wensing-2017 origin of the QDD lineage. Introduces the 'cost of transport' framing as a design figure of merit.
limitations: Early Cheetah — no stable dynamic balance control yet

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: Primary source (IEEE ICRA 2013 abstract + IEEE Spectrum): CONFIRMED COT 0.5 exact quote — 'total power consumption from the battery pack was 973 W and resulted in a total cost of transport of 0.5, which rivals running animals at the same scale.' Book §4.3 claim fully supported.

Reinforcement Learning in Robotics: A Survey
candidates.method_summary: Pre-deep-RL survey of reinforcement-learning techniques in robotics. Useful Ch 2 / Ch 8 bridge showing the state-of-the-art before 2015 and the gap that GPU-parallel sim + deep policies closed.

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: Primary source (Kober IJRR 2013, full PDF via publications.ri.cmu.edu) read. CONFIRMED §2.7 claims: (a) 'tens to hundreds of real-robot trials per learned skill' — Kober §7 ball-in-cup example needs 42-130 episodes on real robot; earlier sim+real example needs 220-300 sim episodes + 40-90 real episodes for adaptation. (b) 'sim-to-real transfer was unreliable' — Kober p42: 'relatively few researchers have successfully demonstrated that a policy learned in simulation can directly be transferred to a real robot while maintaining a high level of performance.' (c) PILCO, PI², REPS — all discussed in the survey as canonical pre-deep-RL policy search methods. Book-writer's §2.7 bullet is fully primary-source-supported.

candidates.title: Capturability-based Analysis and Control of Legged Locomotion, Part 2: Application to M2V2, a Lower-Body Humanoid
candidates.method_summary: M2V2 hardware validation companion to Pratt 2006 Humanoids capture-point paper. Extends capturability formalism with N-step capture regions, then applies the control algorithm to M2V2, a 3D force-controlled lower-body humanoid with 12 actuated leg DoF. Evaluated on push-recovery while single-legged balancing and on walking tasks, both in simulation and on M2V2 hardware. Primary source for the 0.3 m CP-displacement / ~10% body-weight-impulse M2V2 numbers mis-attributed to Pratt 2006.

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: Added to Ch01 references by book-writer 2026-04-24 as M2V2 hardware-validation forward-cite from §1.4 Pratt 2006 correction. Entry confirmed in master bibtex line 3661 and in surveys/humanoid-revolution/book/references.bib. IJRR 31(10):1117-1133 canonical bibliographic data matches. SAGE Journals DOI verified (10.1177/0278364912452762). Fills the M2V2 hardware-lineage gap that the corrected Pratt 2006 citation exposed. ch1 (after §1.2 and §1.4 corrections), Part I — Ch01.

candidates.title: *Feedback Control of Dynamic Bipedal Robot Locomotion*. CRC Press
Capture Point: A Step toward Humanoid Push Recovery
candidates.method_summary: Introduces the capture point (CP) concept: the point on the ground where a biped can step to bring itself to rest, derived from the LIPM orbital energy. Proposes N-step capture regions and uses them for reactive footstep placement in response to pushes. Validated on a Yobotics-sim planar biped with flywheel body. M2V2 hardware validation appears in follow-up Pratt/Koolen 2012 IJRR paper.
limitations: LIPM assumption breaks down under large CoM excursions
Real-world noisy state estimation degrades CP

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: Primary source (IEEE Humanoids 2006, cs.cmu.edu cached PDF) read. M2V2 was NOT in this paper; flywheel + planar biped in Yobotics Simulation Construction Set was. Draft's original 'M2V2 … displace its CP by up to roughly 0.3 m' error was corrected by book-writer on 2026-04-24: §1.4 now reads 'planar bipedal model with a flywheel body (mass 25 kg, inertia 1.225 kg·m², CoM height 0.9375 m, peak ankle torque 100 Nm), recovering from an impulsive push that imparts 0.2 m/s forward velocity via an angular-momentum lunge [Pratt et al., 2006]. Hardware validation on the M2V2 bipedal humanoid followed in Pratt, Koolen, and colleagues' 2012 IJRR paper [Pratt et al., 2012].' Forward-cite to Pratt-Koolen 2012 added to reference list as new entry #3. RESOLVED.

candidates.title: Biped walking pattern generation by using preview control of zero-moment point
candidates.method_summary: Formulates bipedal walking pattern generation as tracking a reference zero-moment-point (ZMP) trajectory with a Linear Inverted Pendulum Model (LIPM) whose center of mass height is held constant. Uses preview control (a discrete-time LQR with a finite preview window of future ZMP references, typically 1.6 s / 1.6× step period) so that the CoM trajectory can anticipate upcoming steps. Demonstrated on HRP-2P with step period around 0.8 s and sinusoidal foot placements.
limitations: Requires precise ZMP reference planned offline
Fails under large unmodeled disturbances or foot-slip
No explicit reasoning about contact uncertainty

survey_backrefs.factcheck_notes: Primary source (mzucker.github.io/swarthmore cached PDF) read. 1.6 s preview window, 5 ms sampling (200 Hz), HRP-2P simulation-only CONFIRMED. Draft's original '0.8 s step period' error was corrected by book-writer on 2026-04-24: §1.2 now reads 'demonstrates the approach in simulation using HRP-2P model parameters (154 cm tall, 58 kg)' and reframes 0.8 s as the preview-window failure case (not the step period). Hardware-validation-as-next-step framing added. 5 ms sampling parenthetical added. RESOLVED.
